questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: The ratio of number of oxygen atoms $(\mathrm{O})$ in $16.0 \mathrm{~g}$ ozone $\left(\mathrm{O}_3\right), 28.0 \mathrm{~g}$ carbon monoxide $(\mathrm{CO})$ and $16.0$ oxygen $\left(\mathrm{O}_2\right)$ is (Atomic mass : $\mathrm{C}=12, \mathrm{O}=16$ and Avogadro's constant $\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}=6.0 \times 10^{23} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ )
questions.solution:
$16.0 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{O}_3=\frac{16}{48}$ mole
$$
\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{16}{48} \times 6.023 \times 10^{23} \text { molecules } \\
& =3 \times \frac{16}{48} \times 6.023 \times 10^{23} \text { atoms } \\
& =6.023 \times 10^{23} \text { atoms } \\
& 28.0 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{CO}=\frac{28}{28} \text { mole }=1 \text { mole } \\
& =1 \times 6.023 \times 10^{23} \text { molecules } \\
& =1 \times 6.023 \times 10^{23} \text { atoms } \\
& =6.023 \times 10^{23} \text { atoms } \\
& 16.0 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{O}_2=\frac{16}{16} \text { mole }=1 \mathrm{~mole}
\end{aligned}
$$
$=1 \times 6.023 \times 10^{23}$ molecules
$=1 \times 6.023 \times 10^{23}$ atoms
$=6.023 \times 10^{23}$ atoms
Therefore, the ratio is
$6.023 \times 10^{23}: 6.023 \times 10^{23}: 6.023 \times 10^{23}$
i.e. $1: 1: 1$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> of NaOH of molarity X and <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><msub><mrow><mo>)</mo></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> of molarity Y are mixed together. The mixture is completely neutralized by 100 mL of 0.1N HCl. If <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>/</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>4</mn></math> and X/Y = 4 then what fraction of acid is neutralized by <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><msub><mrow><mo>)</mo></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math>
questions.solution:
Meq. of NaOH + Meq. of Ba(OH)2=Meq. of HCl
X.V1+2Y.V2=100×0.1=10
or 4Y.V24+2Y.V2=10
or 3YV2=10
2Y.V2=10×23=6.66
Fraction used by Ba(OH)2=6.6610=0.67
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q:
$1.25 \mathrm{~g}$ of a sample of $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ is
dissolved in $250 \mathrm{ml}$ solution. $25 \mathrm{ml}$ of this solution neutralises $20 \mathrm{ml}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$. The $\%$ of $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$
in this sample is
questions.solution:
Let the amount of $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$ present in the mixture be $\mathrm{xg} . \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ will not react with $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} .$ Then
$\frac{x}{53}=\frac{20 \times 0.1 \times 10}{1000} \therefore x=1.06 g$
$\therefore$ Percentage of $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}=$
$\frac{1.06 \times 100}{1.25}=84.8 \%$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: A compound of Xe and $\mathrm{F}$ is found to have $53.5 \%$ of Xe. What is oxidation number of Xe in this compound?
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{Xe}=53.5 \% \therefore \mathrm{F}=46.5 \%$
Relative number of atoms Xe
$=\frac{53.5}{131.2}=0.4 \text { and } \mathrm{F}=\frac{46.5}{19}=2.4$
Simple ratio $\mathrm{Xe}=1$ and $\mathrm{F}=6 ;$ Molecular formula is $\mathrm{XeF}_{6}$
O. $\mathrm{N}$.of $\mathrm{Xe}$ is +6
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: Calculate the number of oxygen atoms required to combine with <math><mn>7</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> to form <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></math> when <math><mn>80</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">%</mi></math> of <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> is converted to <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></math> .
questions.solution:
for formation of N2O3
2N2+3O2→2N2O3
2 moles of N2 combines with 3mole of O2
Given, 7gm N2 (80% only reacts ) i.e.5.6 gm of N2
Mole of N2=5.628=0.2
Hence 20.2=3xmoleofO2
x=0.3 moles
Number of atoms =0.3×2×6.023×1023
=3.61×1023
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

<math><mn>0.07</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi></math>

<math><mn>0.08</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi></math>

<math><mn>0.5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi></math>
questions.solution:
98 g of H2SO4 has g-atoms of S=1 

4.9 g of H2SO4 has =4.998= 0.05 g-atoms of S

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: The molarity of $\mathrm{NO}_{3}{ }^{-}$in the solution after $2 \mathrm{~L}$ of $3 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ is mixed with $3 \mathrm{~L}$ of $1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{BaCl}_{2}$ is
questions.solution:
$2 \mathrm{~L}$ of $3 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ will contains 6 moles of $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$
$3 \mathrm{~L}$ of $1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{BaCl}_{2}$ will contain 3 moles of $\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}$
$2 \mathrm{AgNO}_{3}+\mathrm{BaCl}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{AgCl}$
$+\mathrm{Ba}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}$
So, 6 moles of $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ will react with 3 moles on $\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}$ it means, two solution will react completely to form 3 moles of $\mathrm{Ba}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2} \equiv 6$ moles of $\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}$ions in $2+$ $3=5 \mathrm{~L}$ solution
Hence, molarity of $\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}=\frac{6}{5}=1.2 \mathrm{M}$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: For the reaction $\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}+3 \mathrm{CO} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Fe}+3 \mathrm{CO}_{2}$, the volume of carbon monoxide required to reduce one mole of ferric oxide is
questions.solution:
$$
\underset{1 \mathrm{~mol}}{\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}}+3 \underset{3 \mathrm{~mol}}{3 \mathrm{CO}} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Fe}+3 \mathrm{CO}_{2}
$$
Volume of 1 mole carbon monoxide $=22.4 \mathrm{~L}$ (at STP)
1 mole of ferric oxide is reduced by $=3$ moles of $\mathrm{CO}$
$=3 \times 22.4 \mathrm{~L}$ of $\mathrm{CO}$
$=67.2 \mathrm{dm}^{3}$ of $\mathrm{CO}$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q:
\( 0.30 \mathrm{~g} \) of an organic compound containing \( \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{H} \) and Oxygen on combustion yields \( 0.44 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{CO}_{2} \)
and \( 0.18 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \). If one mol of compound weighs \( 60 \), then molecular formula of the compound
is
questions.solution:
Percentage of $C=\frac{12}{44} \times \frac{0.44}{0.30} \times 100=40 \%$
Percentage of $\mathrm{H}=\frac{2}{18} \times \frac{0.18}{0.30} \times 100=6.6 \%$
Percentage of $O=100-(40+6.6)=53.4 \%$
\begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline Element & \% & Molar ratio & Simplest ratio \\
\hline \mathrm{C} & 40 & \frac{40}{12}=3.3 & \frac{3.3}{3.3}=1 \\
\hline \mathrm{H} & 6.6 & \frac{6.6}{1}=6.6 & \frac{6.6}{3.3}=2 \\
\hline \mathrm{O} & 53.4 & \frac{53.4}{16}=3.3 & \frac{3.3}{3.3}=1 \\
\hline
\end{array}
hence, empirical formula $=\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
We know
$n=\frac{\text { Molecular mass }}{\text { Empirial formula mass }}=\frac{60}{30}=2$ $\Rightarrow$ Molecular formula of compound $=\left(\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}\right)=\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ Hence, molar mass of $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{2}=2 \times 12+1 \times 4+16 \times 2=60 \mathrm{gmol}^{-1}$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q:
What is the mass of the precipitate formed when 50 mL of 16.9% solution of AgNO3 is mixed with 50 mL of 5.8% NaCl solution?
(Ag = 107.8, N = 14, O = 16, Na = 23, Cl = 35.5)
questions.solution:
16.9 g AgNO3 is present in 100 mL solution.
∴8.45 g AgNo3 is present in 50 mL solution
5.8 g NaCl is present in 100 mL solution
∴2.9 g NaCl is present in 50 mL solution

Mass of AgCl
=0.049×143.5 g
=7 g AgCl
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: A metal <math><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> forms the sulphate <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><msub><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub><mo>.</mo></math> A <math><mn>0.596</mn></math> gram sample of the sulphate reacts with excess <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> to give <math><mn>1.220</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msub><mo>.</mo></math> What is the atomic weight of <math> <mtext>M</mtext>  </math>(in g/mol)?
questions.solution:
Mole of BaSO4=1.22233.3
Moles of M2SO43=1.22233.3×13
=1.743×10-3
Wt. of M2SO43=0.596
∴  1.743×10-3 2M+96×3=0.596
M=26.9
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: $100 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $\frac{N}{5} \mathrm{HCl}$ was added to $1 \mathrm{~g}$ of pure $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$. What would remain after the reaction?
questions.solution:
Number of moles of $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}=\frac{W}{M}=\frac{1}{100}$ $=0.01 \mathrm{~mol}$ $\therefore$ Molar mass of $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}=100 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ $100 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $\frac{N}{5} \mathrm{HCl}=\frac{100}{1000} \times \frac{1}{5}=0.02 \mathrm{~mol}$ The reaction is

$\therefore 0.01$ mole of $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$ reacts with $0.02$ mole of $\mathrm{HCl}$. $\therefore$ Both are completely consumed in the given reaction.
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: In an experiment, 2.4 g of iron oxide on reduction with hydrogen gave 1.68 g of iron. In another experiment, 2.9 g of iron oxide gave 2.09 g of iron on reduction. Which law is illustrated from the above data?
questions.solution:
In first experiment,

24 g of iron oxide gave 1.68 g of iron.

Mass of iron oxide =2.4 g

Mass of iron =1.68 g

Mass of oxygen =2.4-1.68=0.72

Ratio of masses of iron and oxygen =1.680.72=7:3

In second experiment,

Mass of iron oxide =2.9 g

Mass of iron =2.09 g

Mass of oxygen =2.9-2.09=0.81

Ratio of masses of iron and oxygen =2.090.81=7:3
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: Pure water can be obtained from various sources, but it always contains hydrogen and oxygen, combined in a ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>8</mn></math> by weight. This is an example of ___________.
questions.solution:
According to law of definite proportion, the compound always contains element in a fixed ratio of their mass.

In water, the mass ratio of hydrogen and oxygen is always fixed whether it is obtained from sea, lake, pond, river, etc. The composition of both hydrogen and oxygen never changes.
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: Mixture of 1 g each of Na<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub> and NaHCO<sub>3</sub> is reacted with 0.1 N HCl. The quantity of 0.1 M HCl required to react completely with the above mixture is
questions.solution:
Na 2 CO 3 + 2 HCl → 2 NaCl + H 2 O + CO 2 1 mol 2 mol

Na 2 CO 3 = 1 g= 1 106 mol.N a 2 C o 3 ≡ 2 106 mol.HCl

NaHCO 3 + HCl → NaCl + H 2 O + CO 2

NaHCO 3 = 1 g = 1 8 4 mol ≡ 1 8 4 mol HCl

Total HCl required = 1 5 3 + 1 8 4 mol HCl

Let volume of 0.1 N HCl = V mL

then, Moles =  0.1 × V 1 0 0 0 = V 1 0 0 0 0

∴   V 1 0 0 00 = 1 5 3 + 1 8 4

            =  0.0308 mol

V = 0.0308 × 1 0 0 0 0

   =  308 mL
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q:
Sulphur trioxide is prepared by the following two reactions:

S8(s)+8O2(g)→8SO2(g)2SO2(g)+O2(g)→2SO3(g)

How many grams of SO3 are produced from 1 mol of S8?
questions.solution:
From the first reaction:

1 mol of S8 → 8 mol of SO2

Hence, SO2 after the first reaction will be 8 moles.

Now, from the second reaction:

2 mol of SO2 → 2 mol of SO3∴ 8 mol of SO2 → 8 mol of SO3

Therefore, the mass of sulphur trioxide produced =Number of moles×Molecular weight=8×80=640 g
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
